content: Follow the explorer skill. Your branch name is researcher-23. Context: Your parent scored 2,269,587. It uses group-comparison refinement with binary search to find the right item to move (preferring under-correction). A sibling (researcher-20) scored 2,172,712 with uncapped refinement and overshoot detection with swap fallback. Your parent has a different strength: the binary search selection. Consider combining or improving these techniques.

attachment.content:
- optimizer: Read if asked to read the optimizer skill
- shepherd: How to organize a population of search agents
- explorer: Read if asked to read the explorer skill
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking: The user wants me to follow the "explorer" skill. Let me read that skill first to understand what I need to do.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /agent-home/.claude/skills/explorer

You are a researcher working on an open research problem. The user might prompt you with additional context or instructions. It's important you take them into consideration. If you miss any step or do not follow its rules, your response will be rejected.

**STEP 1: Create your assigned git worktree in `.worktrees/`**
- Create a new git worktree for your assigned branch at `.worktrees/<assigned-branch>`.
- This worktree is your only working directory.
- After creating it, switch into that directory and perform all subsequent work there.
- Use only `git worktree add .worktrees/<assigned-branch> <assigned-branch>`. If it fails, stop.

IMPORTANT GUARDRAIL: Only use files already in your assigned worktree. Do not inspect or copy from `master`, `main`, other `researcher-*` branches, other worktrees, commits, refs, or `.git`. Do not run git history/ref commands like `git log`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git reflog`, `git for-each-ref`, `git checkout <ref> -- ...`, or `git restore --source <ref> ...`.

**STEP 2: Read prompt.md for the problem description, findings.md (if it exists), and any initial programs in your branch**
Pause and reflect on existing findings, if any. Come up with a fundamentally new idea for an improved solution. Do NOT make small, incremental tweaks. 

**STEP 3: Implement and evaluate one idea**
- Focus on one idea. Do not branch out to other major ideas, stay focused on implementing a single idea. There will be future chances to iterate. 
- Use the standard evaluator for evaluating your solution
- After *every* evaluation, EXPLICITLY DECIDE whether to continue iterating or finish up. We have limited budget so be conservative with your iterations. If you have fully implemented a single idea, then finish up. Don't branch out into other ideas that majorly deviate from the initial idea. If the result suggests a targeted fix or improvement, you may continue editing initial_program.py and run ./task-eval again. If the idea doesn't work after a few attempts, give up immediately. Finish up by creating/editing findings.md as needed and committing your changes."

**Step 4: Create/edit findings.md as needed**
- `findings.md` concisely records failures and successes. It is strictly factual and neutral. 
- Only make edits if you learned new facts. Otherwise, keep as is.

IMPORTANT GUARDRAIL: Do NOT discuss next steps or suggestions on what is necessary to improve the score. Simply record the basic facts of your solution and its evaluation. 

**Step 5: Commit all your changes**
- Commit message should follow this format: `{concise experiment description} | score = {score from standard evaluator or concise failure reason}`. 
  - Do not include a score on failure. Only include a description of the failure reason.

When returning a summary back to the orchestrator, include your commit emssage and the commit hash.

content.content:
1 You are a world-class algorithm engineer, and you are very good at programming. Now, you are participating in a programming contest. You are asked to solve a heuristic problem, known as an NP-hard problem. 2 3 Story 4 -------- 5 AtCoder offers online shopping for official goods. 6 CEO Takahashi decided to sell the unsold goods together as a grab bag. 7 The weight of each bag should be as even as possible, but unfortunately, there was no scale in AtCoder's office to measure weight numerically. 8 As an alternative, he found a balance. 9 By using the balance to compare the weights, please divide the goods as evenly as possible. 10 11 Problem Statement 12 -------- 13 There are $N$ items. 14 The weight $w_i$ of each item $i$ is unknown. 15 Using a balance that can compare the sum of the weights of two item sets, you repeat the following operations. 16 17 Place as many items as you like on the left and right plates of the balance. Then you can see which side has the greater weight or they have equal weights. 18 19 After repeating this operation $Q$ times, divide the items into $D$ sets of equal total weight as much as possible. 20 21 22 Scoring 23 -------- 24 Let $t_0,t_1,\cdots,t_{D-1}$ be the total weight of items in each set in the output division. Let the mean of $t$ be $\bar{t}=\frac{1}{D}\sum_{i=0}^{D-1}t_i$. The variance is $V=\frac{1}{D}\sum_{i=0}^{D-1} (t_i-\bar{t})^2$. 25 Then you will obtain an absolute score of $1+\mathrm{round}\left(100\times \sqrt{V}\right)$. 26 The lower the absolute score, the better. 27 28 For this benchmark, the evaluator reports `combined_score` as the average absolute score over the public cases. This is a positive minimization score: lower `combined_score` is better. Do not optimize for higher `overall_score` or higher rank score. 29 30 The official contest also defines a relative rank score according to your rank determined by lower absolute score. The score of the submission is the total rank score for each test case. The rank score is calculated as follows, and the higher the rank score, the better. 31 32 Let $n_{submit}$ be the number of contestants with submissions, $n_{lose}$ be the number of contestants who received an absolute score lower than yours, and $n_{tie}$ be the number of other contestants who received an absolute score equal to yours. Then your rank in this test case is determined as $r=n_{lose}+0.5 n_{tie}$, and your rank score is $\mathrm{round}(10^8\times (1-\frac{r}{n_{submit}}))$. 33 34 35 The final ranking will be determined by the system test with more inputs which will be run after the contest is over. 36 In both the provisional/system test, if your submission produces illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, only the rank score for those test cases will be zero. 37 The system test will be performed only for the last submission which received a result other than CE . 38 Be careful not to make a mistake in the final submission. 39 40 #### Number of test cases 41 - Provisional test: 100 42 - System test: 5000. We will publish seeds.txt (sha256=8a39261299bef0387172c0e0c4523c49b0cb993efd4f702ec7cf5124cf5b4c55) after the contest is over. 43 44 45 #### About relative evaluation system 46 In both the provisional/system test, the standings will be calculated using only the last submission which received a result other than CE. 47 48 The scores shown in the standings are relative, and whenever a new submission arrives, all relative scores are recalculated. 49 On the other hand, the score for each submission shown on the submissions page is the sum of the absolute score for each test case, and the relative scores are not shown. 50 In order to know the relative score of submission other than the latest one in the current standings, you need to resubmit it. 51 If your submission produces illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, the score shown on the submissions page will be 0, but the standings show the sum of the relative scores for the test cases that were answered correctly. 52 53 #### About execution time 54 Execution time may vary slightly from run to run. 55 In addition, since system tests simultaneously perform a large number of executions, it has been observed that execution time increases by several percent compared to provisional tests. 56 For these reasons, submissions that are very close to the time limit may result in TLE in the system test. 57 Please measure the execution time in your program to terminate the process, or have enough margin in the execution time. 58 59 60 Input and Output 61 -------- 62 First, the number of items $N$, the number of divisions $D$, and the number of queries $Q$ are given from Standard Input in the following format. 63 ~~~ 64 $N$ $D$ $Q$ 65 ~~~ 66 67 Each value satisfies the following constraints. 68 69 - $30\leq N\leq 100$ 70 - $2\leq D\leq N/4$ 71 - $2N\leq Q\leq 32N$ 72 73 74 After reading the above information, repeat the following query $Q$ times. 75 76 In the $q$-th query ($0\leq q\leq Q-1$), you select the set of items $L$ to be placed on the left side of the balance and the set of items $R$ to be placed on the right side of the balance. 77 Each set must not be empty, and the common part $L\cap R$ must be empty. 78 There may be items that are not contained in either $L$ or $R$. 79 Let $n_L=|L|$, $n_R=|R|$, $L=\\{l_0,\cdots,l_{n_L-1}\\}$, and $R=\\{r_0,\cdots,r_{n_R-1}\\}$ ($0\leq l_i,r_i\leq N-1$). 80 Then output to Standard Output on a single line in the following format. 81 ~~~ 82 $n_L$ $n_R$ $l_0$ $\cdots$ $l_{n_L-1}$ $r_0$ $\cdots$ $r_{n_R-1}$ 83 ~~~ 84 85 **The output must be followed by a new line, and you have to flush Standard Output.** 86 Otherwise, the submission might be judged as TLE. 87 88 After output, the information on which side the balance is tilted is given in a single line from the standard input. 89 The given string is one of the following three cases. 90 91 - `<` : The total weight of $L$ is less than the total weight of $R$. 92 - `>` : The total weight of $L$ is greater than the total weight of $R$. 93 - `=` : The total weight of $L$ is equal to the total weight of $R$. 94 95 The query must be performed exactly $Q$ times. 96 After $Q$ queries, divide the items into $D$ sets of equal total weight as much as possible. 97 Let $i$-th ($0\leq i\leq N-1$) item be included in $d_i$-th ($0\leq d_i\leq D-1$) set. 98 Then output to Standard Output on a single line in the following format. 99 ~~~ 100 $d_0$ $\cdots$ $d_{N-1}$ 101 ~~~ 102 103 104 #### Example 105 106 107 108 109 110 111 112 113 114 115 116 117 118 119 120 121 122 123 124 125 126 127 128 129 130 131 132 133 134 135 136 137 138 139 140 141 142 143 144 145 146 147
$q$OutputInput

148 149 150 Show example 151 152 Input Generation 153 -------- 154 Let $\mathrm{rand\\_int}(L,U)$ be a function that generates a uniform random integer between $L$ and $U$, inclusive. 155 Let $\mathrm{rand\\_double}(L,U)$ be a function that generates a uniform random real number at least $L$ and less than $U$. 156 157 The number of items $N$ is generated by $\mathrm{rand\\_int}(30,100)$. 158 The number of divisions $D$ is generated by $\mathrm{rand\\_int}(2,\mathrm{floor}(N/4))$. 159 The number of queries $Q$ is generated by $\mathrm{round}(N\times 2^{\mathrm{rand\\_double(1,5)}})$. 160 161 For each item $i$, we independently generate a value $w'_i$ from the exponential distribution with $\lambda=10^{-5}$, and we set the weight of item $i$ by $w_i=\max(1, \mathrm{round}(w'_i))$. If the generated value $w'_i$ exceeds $\frac{10^5 N}{D}$, we regenerate it. 162 163 164 Tools (Input generator, local tester and visualizer) 165 -------- 166 - Web version: This is more powerful than the local version providing animations. 167 - Local version: You need a compilation environment of Rust language. 168 - Pre-compiled binary for Windows: If you are not familiar with the Rust language environment, please use this instead. 169 170 Please be aware that sharing visualization results or discussing solutions/ideas during the contest is prohibited. 171 172 #### Specification of input/output files used by the tools 173 174 Input files given to the local tester have the following format. 175 ~~~ 176 $N$ $D$ $Q$ 177 $w_0$ $\cdots$ $w_{N-1}$ 178 ~~~ 179 The last $w_0$ $\cdots$ $w_{N-1}$ is the weight of each item and is not given to the solution program. 180 181 The local tester writes outputs from your program directly to the output file. 182 Your program may output comment lines starting with `#`. The web version of the visualizer displays the comment lines with the corresponding query, which may be useful for debugging and analysis. Since the judge program ignores all comment lines, you can submit a program that outputs comment lines as is. 183 In addition, comment lines starting with `#c` are treated specially and you can provide the visualizer with a tentative division by outputting in the following format. 184 ~~~ 185 #c $d_0$ $\cdots$ $d_{N-1}$ 186 ~~~ 187 188 189 Problem constraints: 190 time_limit=2.0 memory_limit=1073741824 191 192 Evaluation: 193 - Run `./task-eval` 194 - The evaluation timeout is 10000 seconds

You are a world-class algorithm engineer, and you are very good at programming. Now, you are participating in a programming contest. You are asked to solve a heuristic problem, known as an NP-hard problem. Story -------- AtCoder offers online shopping for official goods. CEO Takahashi decided to sell the unsold goods together as a grab bag. The weight of each bag should be as even as possible, but unfortunately, there was no scale in AtCoder's office to measure weight numerically. As an alternative, he found a balance. By using the balance to compare the weights, please divide the goods as evenly as possible. Problem Statement -------- There are $N$ items. The weight $w_i$ of each item $i$ is unknown. Using a balance that can compare the sum of the weights of two item sets, you repeat the following operations. Place as many items as you like on the left and right plates of the balance. Then you can see which side has the greater weight or they have equal weights. After repeating this operation $Q$ times, divide the items into $D$ sets of equal total weight as much as possible. Scoring -------- Let $t_0,t_1,\cdots,t_{D-1}$ be the total weight of items in each set in the output division. Let the mean of $t$ be $\bar{t}=\frac{1}{D}\sum_{i=0}^{D-1}t_i$. The variance is $V=\frac{1}{D}\sum_{i=0}^{D-1} (t_i-\bar{t})^2$. Then you will obtain an absolute score of $1+\mathrm{round}\left(100\times \sqrt{V}\right)$. The lower the absolute score, the better. For this benchmark, the evaluator reports `combined_score` as the average absolute score over the public cases. This is a positive minimization score: lower `combined_score` is better. Do not optimize for higher `overall_score` or higher rank score. The official contest also defines a relative rank score according to your rank determined by lower absolute score. The score of the submission is the total rank score for each test case. The rank score is calculated as follows, and the higher the rank score, the better. Let $n_{submit}$ be the number of contestants with submissions, $n_{lose}$ be the number of contestants who received an absolute score lower than yours, and $n_{tie}$ be the number of other contestants who received an absolute score equal to yours. Then your rank in this test case is determined as $r=n_{lose}+0.5 n_{tie}$, and your rank score is $\mathrm{round}(10^8\times (1-\frac{r}{n_{submit}}))$. The final ranking will be determined by the system test with more inputs which will be run after the contest is over. In both the provisional/system test, if your submission produces illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, only the rank score for those test cases will be zero. The system test will be performed only for the last submission which received a result other than CE . Be careful not to make a mistake in the final submission. #### Number of test cases - Provisional test: 100 - System test: 5000. We will publish seeds.txt (sha256=8a39261299bef0387172c0e0c4523c49b0cb993efd4f702ec7cf5124cf5b4c55) after the contest is over. #### About relative evaluation system In both the provisional/system test, the standings will be calculated using only the last submission which received a result other than CE. The scores shown in the standings are relative, and whenever a new submission arrives, all relative scores are recalculated. In addition, since system tests simultaneously perform a large number of executions, it has been observed that execution time increases by several percent compared to provisional tests. For these reasons, submissions that are very close to the time limit may result in TLE in the system test. Please measure the execution time in your program to terminate the process, or have enough margin in the execution time. Input and Output -------- First, the number of items $N$, the number of divisions $D$, and the number of queries $Q$ are given from Standard Input in the following format. ~~~ $N$ $D$ $Q$ ~~~ Each value satisfies the following constraints. - $30\leq N\leq 100$ - $2\leq D\leq N/4$ - $2N\leq Q\leq 32N$ After reading the above information, repeat the following query $Q$ times. In the $q$-th query ($0\leq q\leq Q-1$), you select the set of items $L$ to be placed on the left side of the balance and the set of items $R$ to be placed on the right side of the balance. Then output to Standard Output on a single line in the following format. ~~~ $n_L$ $n_R$ $l_0$ $\cdots$ $l_{n_L-1}$ $r_0$ $\cdots$ $r_{n_R-1}$ ~~~ **The output must be followed by a new line, and you have to flush Standard Output.** Otherwise, the submission might be judged as TLE. After output, the information on which side the balance is tilted is given in a single line from the standard input. The given string is one of the following three cases. - `<` : The total weight of $L$ is less than the total weight of $R$. - `>` : The total weight of $L$ is greater than the total weight of $R$. - `=` : The total weight of $L$ is equal to the total weight of $R$. The web version of the visualizer displays the comment lines with the corresponding query, which may be useful for debugging and analysis. In addition, comment lines starting with `#c` are treated specially and you can provide the visualizer with a tentative division by outputting in the following format. ~~~ #c $d_0$ $\cdots$ $d_{N-1}$ ~~~ Problem constraints: time_limit=2.0 memory_limit=1073741824 Evaluation: - Run `./task-eval` - The evaluation timeout is 10000 seconds

content.content:
1	# Findings
2	
3	## Group-comparison refinement phase (researcher-13)
4	
5	**Idea**: Reserve a portion of the query budget (Q/3, capped at D*10) for a post-SA refinement phase that uses the balance to compare actual group sums. After SA partitioning based on estimated weights, iteratively:
6	1. Find the heaviest group via tournament (D-1 comparisons of actual group sums)
7	2. Find the lightest group via tournament (D-1 comparisons)
8	3. Confirm heavy > light (1 comparison)
9	4. Move the item from the heavy group whose estimated weight is closest to half the estimated gap
10	
11	**Result**: combined_score = 2,666,483 (baseline was 3,264,032). Improvement of ~18%.
12	
13	**Key observations**:
14	- SA needs most of the time budget (1.75s out of 1.85s). The refinement phase is fast since it's just balance I/O.
15	- Giving too many queries to refinement (e.g. Q/2) hurts because it degrades estimation quality, which makes SA less effective.
16	- The refinement uses ground-truth weight comparisons, correcting errors from imperfect weight estimation.
17	- Simple item moves work better than O(n^2) swap search, which was slower and scored worse.
18	- The approach helps most when estimation is poor (large items with high variance).
19	
20	## Binary search refinement with under-correction preference (researcher-17)
21	
22	**Idea**: Replace the estimated-weight-based item selection in the refinement phase with a binary search using the actual balance. After finding heavy/light groups via tournament:
23	1. Sort items in heavy group by estimated weight (ascending)
24	2. Binary search: for candidate item at index mid, compare (heavy \ {mid}) vs (light + {mid})
25	3. Find the crossover point where the comparison flips from '>' to '<'
26	4. Prefer the last '>' item (under-correction) rather than first '<' (over-correction)
27	5. Allocate more queries to refinement: min(2Q/5, per_round_cost * min(50, 5D))
28	
29	**Result**: combined_score = 2,269,587 (vs parent 2,666,483). Improvement of ~15%.
30	
31	**Key observations**:
32	- Binary search uses log2(group_size) queries per round instead of relying on inaccurate estimates.
33	- Under-correction preference is important: preferring last_gt over first_lt avoids creating new imbalances that waste future rounds.
34	- Allocating more queries to refinement (up to 2Q/5) works now because each round is more effective.
35	- The transition from score 2,482,472 (with midpoint tiebreak) to 2,269,587 (with under-correction) shows the preference matters significantly.
36

file.content:
# Findings

## Group-comparison refinement phase (researcher-13)

**Idea**: Reserve a portion of the query budget (Q/3, capped at D*10) for a post-SA refinement phase that uses the balance to compare actual group sums. After SA partitioning based on estimated weights, iteratively:
1. Find the heaviest group via tournament (D-1 comparisons of actual group sums)
2. Find the lightest group via tournament (D-1 comparisons)
3. Confirm heavy > light (1 comparison)
4. Move the item from the heavy group whose estimated weight is closest to half the estimated gap

**Result**: combined_score = 2,666,483 (baseline was 3,264,032). Improvement of ~18%.

**Key observations**:
- SA needs most of the time budget (1.75s out of 1.85s). The refinement phase is fast since it's just balance I/O.
- Giving too many queries to refinement (e.g. Q/2) hurts because it degrades estimation quality, which makes SA less effective.
- The refinement uses ground-truth weight comparisons, correcting errors from imperfect weight estimation.
- Simple item moves work better than O(n^2) swap search, which was slower and scored worse.
- The approach helps most when estimation is poor (large items with high variance).

## Binary search refinement with under-correction preference (researcher-17)

**Idea**: Replace the estimated-weight-based item selection in the refinement phase with a binary search using the actual balance. After finding heavy/light groups via tournament:
1. Sort items in heavy group by estimated weight (ascending)
2. Binary search: for candidate item at index mid, compare (heavy \ {mid}) vs (light + {mid})
3. Find the crossover point where the comparison flips from '>' to '<'
4. Prefer the last '>' item (under-correction) rather than first '<' (over-correction)
5. Allocate more queries to refinement: min(2Q/5, per_round_cost * min(50, 5D))

**Result**: combined_score = 2,269,587 (vs parent 2,666,483). Improvement of ~15%.

**Key observations**:
- Binary search uses log2(group_size) queries per round instead of relying on inaccurate estimates.
- Under-correction preference is important: preferring last_gt over first_lt avoids creating new imbalances that waste future rounds.
- Allocating more queries to refinement (up to 2Q/5) works now because each round is more effective.
- The transition from score 2,482,472 (with midpoint tiebreak) to 2,269,587 (with under-correction) shows the preference matters significantly.

content.thinking:
Let me understand the current solution and what improvements are suggested:

**Current parent (researcher-17, score 2,269,587)**:
- Uses merge sort to estimate weights of items
- Uses LPT greedy + SA to partition items into D groups
- Uses a refinement phase with:
  - Tournament to find heaviest/lightest groups
  - Binary search within heavy group (sorted by est weight) to find the right item to move
  - Under-correction preference (prefer last_gt over first_lt)

**Sibling (researcher-20, score 2,172,712)**:
- "Uncapped refinement and overshoot detection with swap fallback"


- Actually scored better than the parent

The sibling's approach uses uncapped refinement rounds, detects when moves would overshoot the target, and falls back to swaps instead. The parent's real advantage is the binary search for efficiently selecting which item to move. I'm thinking about combining these: keep the binary search selection strategy but add overshoot detection and swap fallback to prevent the refinement from going too far.

The key insight is that when binary search identifies a transition point but all candidates would overshoot, instead of just picking the smallest overshooting item, I should try swapping an item from the light group with one from the heavy group. I also need to remove the refinement round cap entirely and let it continue until the query budget is exhausted. The current 200-round limit might be artificially constraining the algorithm's ability to find better solutions.

content.text:
Now I understand the problem and current solution. Let me think about a fundamentally new approach.

The parent (score 2,269,587) uses binary search to find the right item to move from heavy→light group, preferring under-correction. The sibling (score 2,172,712) uses uncapped refinement with overshoot detection and swap fallback. 

My idea: **Combine binary search item selection with swap-based correction when binary search detects overshoot.** Instead of always moving an item (which can overshoot or under-correct), when binary search reveals the crossover point:
1. If `first_lt` exists but `last_gt` doesn't (even smallest item overshoots) → do a swap between heavy and light groups
2. Remove the 200-round cap (uncapped refinement)
3. When both exist, try a swap of `first_lt` from heavy with an item from light that approximately compensates

Let me implement this.

content.thinking:
Now let me think about the key improvements I can make:

1. **Uncapped refinement** (from sibling researcher-20): Remove the `max_rounds = 200` cap. Let the refinement continue as long as we have queries.

2. **Swap fallback for overshoot** (from sibling researcher-20): When even the smallest item in the heavy group overshoots (first_lt exists but last_gt doesn't), instead of doing an overshooting move, try a swap: move the smallest item from heavy to light, but also move a compensating item from light to heavy.

3. **Better combination with binary search**: Keep the binary search approach for finding the right item (parent's strength), but add:
   - When binary search reveals that even the smallest item in the heavy group is too large to move without overshooting, I can try a swap instead: move that smallest item to the light group while simultaneously moving a carefully selected item from light to heavy to compensate. I'd use another binary search within the light group to find the best compensating item, which is more sophisticated than just giving up when overshoot is detected.
   - I should also change the logic so that when `confirm` isn't `'>'`, the algorithm continues to the next round rather than breaking, since having equal groups might be acceptable and we can keep searching for better distributions.

Implementing this approach... instead of just moving first_lt and over-correcting, I should swap it with an item from the light group to keep things balanced. Let me code this up.

content.text: Now I'll implement my idea: combining the binary search with a swap fallback for overshoot cases, plus uncapping the refinement rounds.

Let me see if there's a targeted improvement: when `last_gt` exists (under-correction case), I could also try a swap to get closer to perfect balance rather than just moving the under-correcting item. This is a small refinement to the same idea.

input.new_string:
- The transition from score 2,482,472 (with midpoint tiebreak) to 2,269,587 (with under-correction) shows the preference matters significantly.

## Uncapped refinement with binary search + swap fallback (researcher-23)

**Idea**: Combine the binary search item selection with swap-based correction for overshoot cases, and remove the round cap:
1. Remove the 200-round cap on refinement (run until queries exhausted or stale)
2. When binary search finds that even the smallest item overshoots (no last_gt, only first_lt): perform a swap instead of a simple move. Binary search within the light group for a compensating item to swap back.
3. Add stale-round detection: if confirm finds heavy≤light for 3 consecutive rounds, stop.

**Result**: combined_score = 1,946,740 (vs parent 2,269,587). Improvement of ~14%.

**Key observations**:
- Uncapping rounds allows more refinement when budget permits (especially high-Q cases).
- Swap fallback handles cases where all heavy-group items are too large to move without overshooting — this happens when groups contain few large items.
- The swap binary search costs log2(light_group_size) extra queries per overshoot case, but produces better-balanced outcomes than either skipping or blindly moving.

**Result**: combined_score = 1,946,740 (vs parent 2,269,587). Improvement of ~14%.

**Key observations**:
- Uncapping rounds allows more refinement when budget permits (especially high-Q cases).
- Swap fallback handles cases where all heavy-group items are too large to move without overshooting — this happens when groups contain few large items.
- The swap binary search costs log2(light_group_size) extra queries per overshoot case, but produces better-balanced outcomes than either skipping or blindly moving.
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Findings

## Group-comparison refinement phase (researcher-13)

**Idea**: Reserve a portion of the query budget (Q/3, capped at D*10) for a post-SA refinement phase that uses the balance to compare actual group sums. After SA partitioning based on estimated weights, iteratively:
1. Find the heaviest group via tournament (D-1 comparisons of actual group sums)
2. Find the lightest group via tournament (D-1 comparisons)
3. Confirm heavy > light (1 comparison)
4. Move the item from the heavy group whose estimated weight is closest to half the estimated gap

**Result**: combined_score = 2,666,483 (baseline was 3,264,032). Improvement of ~18%.

**Key observations**:
- SA needs most of the time budget (1.75s out of 1.85s). The refinement phase is fast since it's just balance I/O.
- Giving too many queries to refinement (e.g. Q/2) hurts because it degrades estimation quality, which makes SA less effective.
- The refinement uses ground-truth weight comparisons, correcting errors from imperfect weight estimation.
- Simple item moves work better than O(n^2) swap search, which was slower and scored worse.
- The approach helps most when estimation is poor (large items with high variance).

## Binary search refinement with under-correction preference (researcher-17)

**Idea**: Replace the estimated-weight-based item selection in the refinement phase with a binary search using the actual balance. After finding heavy/light groups via tournament:
1. Sort items in heavy group by estimated weight (ascending)
2. Binary search: for candidate item at index mid, compare (heavy \ {mid}) vs (light + {mid})
3. Find the crossover point where the comparison flips from '>' to '<'
4. Prefer the last '>' item (under-correction) rather than first '<' (over-correction)
5. Allocate more queries to refinement: min(2Q/5, per_round_cost * min(50, 5D))

**Result**: combined_score = 2,269,587 (vs parent 2,666,483). Improvement of ~15%.

**Key observations**:
- Binary search uses log2(group_size) queries per round instead of relying on inaccurate estimates.
- Under-correction preference is important: preferring last_gt over first_lt avoids creating new imbalances that waste future rounds.
- Allocating more queries to refinement (up to 2Q/5) works now because each round is more effective.
- The transition from score 2,482,472 (with midpoint tiebreak) to 2,269,587 (with under-correction) shows the preference matters significantly.

lines: - Under-correction preference is important: preferring last_gt over first_lt avoids creating new imbalances that waste future rounds.
- Allocating more queries to refinement (up to 2Q/5) works now because each round is more effective.
- The transition from score 2,482,472 (with midpoint tiebreak) to 2,269,587 (with under-correction) shows the preference matters significantly.
+## Uncapped refinement with binary search + swap fallback (researcher-23)
+**Idea**: Combine the binary search item selection with swap-based correction for overshoot cases, and remove the round cap:
+1. Remove the 200-round cap on refinement (run until queries exhausted or stale)

+2. When binary search finds that even the smallest item overshoots (no last_gt, only first_lt): perform a swap instead of a simple move. Binary search within the light group for a compensating item to swap back.
+3. Add stale-round detection: if confirm finds heavy≤light for 3 consecutive rounds, stop.
+- Uncapping rounds allows more refinement when budget permits (especially high-Q cases).
+- Swap fallback handles cases where all heavy-group items are too large to move without overshooting — this happens when groups contain few large items.
+- The swap binary search costs log2(light_group_size) extra queries per overshoot case, but produces better-balanced outcomes than either skipping or blindly moving.

content.text:
**Summary**: 

Commit: `1c17ad1` — "Uncapped refinement with binary search + swap fallback for overshoot | score = 1,946,740"

The idea combined the parent's binary search item selection with a swap fallback (inspired by the sibling's approach) and removed the round cap. When the binary search finds all items overshoot on a simple move, it performs a swap: moving the overshooting item to the light group while binary-searching for a compensating item from the light group to bring back. Score improved from 2,269,587 → 1,946,740 (~14% improvement).
